title: ISS ID-collision class (29 duplicate IDs in closed-issues.json) — retroactive dedup + next-ID intake hardening + daily duplicate-ID integrity audit
author: tinker (DIRECTIVE-20260528-001 execution, run tinker-2026-05-29T02-40)
directive_reference: DIRECTIVE-20260528-001 — Author PROP-063 — closed-issues.json ID-collision class (29 duplicate IDs) and decider next-ID intake hardening. Filed 2026-05-28T05:30Z by operator-cowork-session, priority=low.
predecessor_reference: PROP-062 'failure_pattern_d_iss_id_collision_separate_class' explicitly punted this class to PROP-063 — quote: 'ID-collision is a separate defect class in the decider's next-ID intake (probably driven by partial-run state on 2026-05-18 REPAIR cycle when multiple decider runs touched closed-issues.json near-simultaneously). Recommend separate PROP-063 if operator wants a systemic fix; this PROP only flags the issue and recommends per-entry disposition.'
source_finding: 29 duplicate ID rows in monitor/decisions/closed-issues.json: 4 IDs with 3 entries each (ISS-2120, 2121, 2122, 2123) and 25 IDs with 2 entries each. The 4 three-entry IDs cluster on the 2026-05-18 REPAIR cycle (workspace-sync mass-delete disaster recovery). The 25 two-entry IDs are spread across 2026-05-02 → 2026-05-26 — multiple distinct allocation events, not a single disaster artifact. At least one entry (ISS-2122 entry 1, live-state cleanup pipeline bug) carries a misrouted closure_reason that describes a DIFFERENT entry's patch — the underlying bug may be unfixed.
summary: Per Step 1 diagnosis: 22 of 29 IDs are pure collisions (true different bugs sharing an ID), 4 are full retroactive duplicates (same bug filed twice — safe dedup), 2 are 3-entry hybrids (2 entries are same-bug duplicates + 1 entry is a collision), and 1 (ISS-2122) is a 3-entry pure-collision with a misrouted closure_reason on entry 1. The structural root cause is the decider's next-ID allocator (canonically documented in monitor/prompts/reference/decider-reporting.md Step 5: 'Issue ID Assignment') which reads only open-issues.json.next_id and increments it — it does NOT cross-check max(ID) across closed-issues.json or closure-ledger.jsonl. When the 2026-05-18 mass-delete rewound open-issues.json (and its next_id) to a pre-disaster state, but closed-issues.json was re-populated from rescue commits, the allocator started reissuing IDs that closed-issues.json had already used. The 2-entry clusters from May 2-6 likely have a different proximate cause (multiple decider runs touching closed-issues.json in parallel, or a partial-run rollback where the allocator wrote without the closed-side knowing) — but the fix is the same: make the allocator's max-ID computation walk BOTH open-issues.json AND closed-issues.json AND closure-ledger.jsonl. Mirror the PROP-022 phase-5 archive-aware self-heal pattern that already protects EXP allocation (decider-curmudgeon.md lines 132-147). Add a daily integrity audit (structure-integrity.md) that walks closed-issues.json for duplicate IDs, classified MAJOR (same severity as EXP collisions per PROP-022). Optional same-run guard: at allocation time, the decider can grep the proposed ISS-{N} against closed-issues.json — if found, flag MAJOR and pick max+1 instead.

structural_root_cause.canonical_location: monitor/prompts/reference/decider-reporting.md Step 5 ('Update Issue Tracker' → 'CRITICAL: Issue ID Assignment')
structural_root_cause.current_pseudocode: const o=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('monitor/decisions/open-issues.json','utf8')); const id='ISS-'+o.next_id; o.next_id++; o.issues.push({id, ...}); fs.writeFileSync(...)
structural_root_cause.defect: next_id is sourced exclusively from open-issues.json. closed-issues.json and closure-ledger.jsonl are never consulted. When open-issues.json is rewound (mass-delete recovery, manual edit, rollback) the allocator silently re-allocates IDs that already exist in closed-issues.json. The existing prose 'Never scan for max ID across issues' was added (per the inline comment) because 'IDs were historically reused between open and closed files, causing collisions' — but the *fix* it documents (use next_id from open-issues.json) is the same source of the current collisions. The historical reason for that prose was rate-of-write (every scan is expensive); after PROP-022 phase 5, the EXP allocator demonstrates that a global max-ID walk (live + archive) is cheap enough to run on every allocation and prevents exactly this defect.

proximate_cause_candidates_per_cluster.may_18_cluster_ISS_2120_2123: Workspace-sync mass-delete disaster (2026-05-21 per CLAUDE.md, but the issue cluster's 2026-05-18 source dates suggest the original disaster window was wider, or there was a precursor mass-delete on 2026-05-18). open-issues.json was rewound to pre-disaster; closed-issues.json was re-populated by rescue commits with IDs ≥ the rewound open-side next_id. Decider's next intake run allocated colliding IDs.
proximate_cause_candidates_per_cluster.may_2_6_cluster_ISS_1819_1868: Less clear. Hypotheses: (a) two decider runs near-simultaneously (operator-triggered + cron-triggered) both allocated from the same next_id base before one wrote back, (b) a partial-run rollback where the decider closed an ISS into closed-issues.json but failed to push back open-issues.json's incremented next_id, causing the next run to re-allocate, (c) Mech-A patch path that closed an open ISS via direct write to closed-issues.json without touching open-issues.next_id. The fact that the May-2-6 collisions are pairs (not triples) and span 16 consecutive ISS numbers (1857-1868) is consistent with a single decider run that re-allocated a contiguous block.
proximate_cause_candidates_per_cluster.may_25_26_cluster_ISS_2200_2203_2173: Consistent with PROP-059 mandatory-pending-verification deployment churn + integrity-2026-05-26T01-16 EXP-432 collision discovery. The decider was processing high-volume close-and-collide self-applies during that period; the existing allocator gave it no guardrail.

observable_now: Operator-confusing closed-issues.json queries — grep 'id.*ISS-2122' closed-issues.json returns 3 entries with different descriptions

ISS-2122 entry 1 has a misrouted closure_reason — the underlying 'live-state cleanup pipeline' bug may have been falsely closed (PROP-062 raised this and recommended operator investigation, deferred to PROP-063 for systemic fix)
Verifier (PROP-014 Mechanism 1a verify-pending-state.js) and integrity audits index by id — duplicate-id rows confuse downstream consumers
future_blast_radius_if_unfixed: Next mass-rewind event (FUSE workspace-sync incident, manual rollback, partial-run abort) reintroduces the defect with no detector
Operator time per query grows as the duplicate-ID count compounds — each unique ID resolution requires manual inspection
Any audit that asserts ID uniqueness (e.g. a future integrity check on closed-issues.json) starts firing inconsistently

step_1_per_collision_class_diagnosis.purpose: Per directive's Step 1 — for each of the 29 IDs, classify as (a) same-bug retroactive duplicate, (b) true ID-collision (different bugs), or (c) misrouted closure (closure_reason describes a different entry's patch).

class_legend.A: Same underlying bug — legit retroactive duplicate, candidate for dedup
class_legend.B: Different bugs sharing an ID — true collision, needs ID-rewrite
class_legend.C: Misrouted closure — closure_reason describes a different entry's patch (underlying bug may be unfixed)
class_legend.A_hybrid: Multi-entry where some entries are same-bug duplicates + at least one is a B collision

ISS-2122.disposition: Keep E0 as ISS-2122 (own legit close). Rewrite E2 to a new ID (own legit close). For E1: three operator-decidable options per directive's Step 3 (a) framing: (a1) strip E1's misrouted closure_reason+status, restore to status='assigned-tinker' so the live-state cleanup pipeline bug returns to the tinker queue with a NEW ID; (a2) operator investigates EXP-426 downstream history — grep closed-issues for any subsequent fix of the same bug-class, and if found, repoint E1's closure_reason to cite that fix commit; (a3) mark E1 closed-duplicate/closed-superseded with a pointer to a downstream PROP/EXP that handled the live-state pipeline bug. RECOMMENDED: (a2) — operator should grep EXP-426 + 'live-state cleanup' + 'attention-inbox' against closed-issues to determine if a legitimate downstream fix exists; if not, fall back to (a1).

two_entry_classification_summary.class_C_misrouted_closures_in_2_entry_se: None — the only C-class misrouted closure in the 29-ID set is ISS-2122 entry 1 (in the 3-entry cluster).
underlying_bugs_potentially_unfixed: ISS-2122 entry 1 — live-state cleanup pipeline bug (proposal-NEW-live-state-cleanup-bug-2026-05-18.json, EXP-426 lineage). Closure_reason describes ISS-2122 entry 2's llms.txt patch. Must be investigated and reopened or repointed.

step_2_structural_fix_design.purpose: Per directive's Step 2 — make the next-ID allocator read both open and closed sources, and add a daily duplicate-ID integrity audit. Mirror the PROP-022 phase 5 EXP archive-aware self-heal pattern that already exists.

change_2a_decider_reporting_md_step_5_al.target_section: Step 5: Update Issue Tracker → 'CRITICAL: Issue ID Assignment'
change_2a_decider_reporting_md_step_5_al.current_code_block: const o=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('monitor/decisions/open-issues.json','utf8')); const id='ISS-'+o.next_id; o.next_id++; o.issues.push({id, ...}); fs.writeFileSync(...)
new_code_block_replacement: // PROP-063 archive-aware self-heal: walk closed-issues + closure-ledger to validate next_id
try { const e=JSON.parse(l); const m=(e.iss||e.id||'').match(/^ISS-(\d+)$/); if(m) maxClosedNum=Math.max(maxClosedNum, parseInt(m[1],10)); } catch(_){}
console.warn('next_id self-heal: open.next_id='+o.next_id+', max(open_ids)='+maxOpenNum+', max(closed_ids)='+maxClosedNum+', SAFE='+safeNext);
change_2a_decider_reporting_md_step_5_al.rationale: Mirrors PROP-022 phase 5 EXP archive-aware self-heal (decider-curmudgeon.md lines 132-147). The cost is one extra read of closed-issues.json + closure-ledger.jsonl (both already in the decider's working set for other Step 1 operations). The next_id field remains the canonical write-back path so no schema change to open-issues.json is needed; we only add a defensive read before each allocation.
change_2a_decider_reporting_md_step_5_al.prose_addendum_after_existing_warning: **PROP-063 (2026-05-29):** The 'never scan for max ID across issues' rule from the prior commit was correct in spirit but mis-applied — it forbade a max-ID scan ON ALLOCATION, when the actual defect was failing to cross-check closed-issues.json at all. The PROP-063 self-heal runs an archive-aware MAX walk (open ∪ closed ∪ closure-ledger) and clamps `o.next_id` upward if it lags. The canonical write-back path is still `o.next_id++` — we never decrement, never reuse, and never silently allocate below max-existing-ID. Rationale: the 2026-05-18 mass-delete and several other 2026-05 cluster events demonstrated that open-issues.json.next_id can be rewound by upstream rollback/recovery; the only safe defense is a max-walk at allocation time.

change_2b_decider_intake_md_step_5_cross.edit: Change to: 'Increment `next_id` per Step 5 conventions in decider-reporting.md (PROP-063 archive-aware self-heal — DO NOT bump without running the max(open ∪ closed ∪ closure-ledger) walk).'
change_2b_decider_intake_md_step_5_cross.rationale: The prompt currently dangles a 'Step 5 conventions' reference. PROP-063 makes the convention explicit and cites the canonical location to prevent future confusion about which 'Step 5' is canonical.

change_2c_daily_integrity_audit_for_dupl.new_audit_check_prose: **ISS ID-collision audit (PROP-063, 2026-05-29):** Walk `monitor/decisions/closed-issues.json` for duplicate `id` values. Any duplicate is MAJOR severity (mirrors EXP-tracker duplicate-ID classification — PROP-022 phase 5). Also walk `open-issues.json` and verify no ID appears in BOTH open and closed (cross-file collision). Suggested fix template: 'rewrite duplicate-ID entries to new ISS numbers per PROP-063 retroactive dedup table; investigate any misrouted closure_reasons (where closure_reason describes a different entry's patch than the entry's own description).' If the duplicate-ID audit returns >0 entries that were not already tracked under a PROP-063-class ISS, file a new ISS with category='id_integrity_finding' and severity=major.
new_audit_pseudocode: description:'ISS duplicate-ID rows in closed-issues.json: '+dupes.length+' IDs affected ('+dupes.slice(0,3).map(d=>d[0]).join(', ')+(dupes.length>3?', ...':'')+')',
suggested_fix:'rewrite per PROP-063 retroactive dedup table; investigate misrouted closure_reasons',
description:'ISS cross-file collision (id in both open AND closed): '+crossFile.slice(0,3).join(', ')+(crossFile.length>3?', +'+(crossFile.length-3)+' more':''),
suggested_fix:'allocator hardening per PROP-063 + per-entry rewrite',

change_2d_optional_same_run_decider_guar.purpose: Belt-and-braces: even with the archive-aware self-heal, a same-run collision could happen if (a) decider opens an ISS-{N} that closed-issues.json already contains, (b) decider closes that ISS in the same run. The self-heal prevents (a), but a same-run collision guard is cheap and provides defense in depth.
guard_pseudocode: // At the moment of writing the new ISS into open-issues.json, before push:
console.error('CRITICAL: about to allocate '+proposedId+' which already exists in closed-issues.json. Bumping by 1 and retrying.');
// re-derive id and retry, with a small loop cap (≤5 iterations)
change_2d_optional_same_run_decider_guar.rationale: Cheap, idempotent, only runs at allocation. If the self-heal block above is wired correctly, this guard should never fire — its presence is purely so that we'd LOUDLY know if it did.

step_3_retroactive_dedup_recommendations.purpose: Per directive's Step 3 — for each duplicate ID, recommend dedup-or-rewrite. Operator applies after reviewing.
step_3_retroactive_dedup_recommendations.non_goal: NOT batch-executed. Operator applies (and may choose a different disposition than recommended). This PROP only proposes.

id_renumbering_method.rule: All E1/E2 rewrites should allocate from the post-fix next_id counter (which after PROP-063 Change 2a is guaranteed to be > max(open ∪ closed ∪ closure-ledger)). Suggested batch: 28 new IDs needed total (1 per B/B+C/B+near-dup entry to rewrite + 0 for A-class drops). Allocate them sequentially after applying Change 2a so the self-heal sets next_id correctly.
id_renumbering_method.audit_trail: Each rewritten entry should carry a new field `rewritten_from: 'ISS-NNNN-entry-N'` so future audits can trace the rewrite. Add this field to the recommended-edit script.
id_renumbering_method.old_id_handling_in_closed_issues_json: Two options: (i) DELETE the duplicate entry from closed-issues.json (clean cut, no trace); (ii) REWRITE in place (preserve audit trail). Recommended: (ii) — set `id: 'ISS-{nextAvail}'`, append `rewritten_from: 'ISS-NNNN-collision-entry-N'`. Option (ii) is consistent with the project's 'no destructive modify without audit trail' default.

step_4_operator_cost_vs_benefit_analysis.purpose: Per directive's Step 4 — estimate operator time and recommend integrated vs separate execution.

per_collision_dispositions_cost.rolled_up_human_readable: ~1h 45m for full per-entry execution across all 29 IDs.

components.4_three_entry: (2×A_hybrid ≈ 10 min) + (1×B+C with investigation ≈ 15 min) + (1×B+near-dup ≈ 8 min) = ~33 min
components.total: ~104 minutes operator time for dedup-only path with no structural fix

structural_fix_cost.change_2a_allocator_hardening: ~10 min prompt edit + ~5 min test (run a synthetic mass-delete simulation and verify the self-heal kicks in)
structural_fix_cost.change_2c_daily_integrity_audit: ~10 min prompt edit + ~5 min review of next morning's integrity report to confirm the new audit fires
structural_fix_cost.change_2d_same_run_guard: ~5 min prompt edit (or skip — Changes 2a+2c provide enough defense without it)
structural_fix_cost.total_structural_only: ~25-35 min operator time + first verification cycle (24h wait for next integrity run)

combined_recommendation.do_both_in_phased_order: Phase 1 (~30 min): Apply Changes 2a, 2b, 2c first. This prevents future collisions and surfaces any existing ones via the daily audit — confirming the 29 known IDs and revealing any others that may exist. Phase 2 (~105 min): Apply per-ID dedup recommendations from Step 3, ordered by class (4 A-class quick wins first, then 21 B-class rewrites, then 4 hybrid/C-class with investigation last). Phase 3 (~10 min): Optional Change 2d if Phase 1 fix alone leaves any anxiety.
combined_recommendation.alternative_a_structural_only: Apply Changes 2a-c only. Leave the 29 existing duplicate IDs as-is. Pros: 30 min total, prevents future occurrences, daily audit gives ongoing visibility. Cons: ISS-2122 entry 1's potentially-unfixed live-state cleanup pipeline bug remains in misrouted state; closed-issues queries still return ambiguous results for affected IDs.
combined_recommendation.alternative_b_dedup_only: Apply per-ID dedup, skip structural fix. Pros: cleans up current state. Cons: 105 min one-shot, the underlying defect remains so the next mass-delete or partial-run rollback reintroduces it. DO NOT recommend.
combined_recommendation.recommended: Phase 1 + Phase 2 (~135 min total operator time spread across however many sessions operator prefers; Phase 1 is self-contained and Phase 2 can be deferred indefinitely once Phase 1 is in place). Phase 1 is the higher-leverage half by a wide margin — apply it first.

step_4_operator_cost_vs_benefit_analysis.highest_priority_action: ISS-2122 entry 1 — live-state cleanup pipeline bug investigation. This is the only one of the 29 collision rows with a credible chance of representing UNFIXED WORK. Operator should grep EXP-426 + 'live-state cleanup' against closed-issues + analyst-reports to determine current state before applying any other Phase 2 action.

alternatives_considered.scan_max_id_on_every_allocation_with_no_: Pros: simplest mental model, no state to corrupt. Cons: O(N) on every allocation where N is closed-issues growth (~2,100 currently, growing). The existing next_id counter pattern is a cheap O(1) optimization; PROP-063's self-heal preserves that fast-path and only does the max-walk as a defensive cross-check.
alternatives_considered.lock_file_or_advisory_lock_during_alloca: Pros: prevents the cross-clone race for the May-2-6 cluster cause. Cons: lock files don't survive FUSE quirks; advisory locks add cross-agent complexity. The archive-aware max-walk handles ALL the observed causes (mass-delete rewind, cross-clone race, partial-run rollback) with one mechanism, so a lock is redundant.
alternatives_considered.remove_next_id_field_entirely_force_max_: Pros: eliminates the rewind footgun. Cons: changes the open-issues.json schema, breaks the existing 'CRITICAL: Issue ID Assignment' contract, requires migration script. The PROP-063 self-heal achieves the same safety with no schema change.
alternatives_considered.do_nothing_treat_collisions_as_acceptabl: Per directive: 'The 29 duplicate IDs are not actively breaking anything — verifier audits, integrity audits, and routine pipeline operations all work despite the duplicates.' True, but PROP-062 already verified one case (ISS-2122) where a duplicate ID + misrouted closure may have falsely closed an underlying bug. The cost of doing-nothing is unbounded — every future mass-rewind reintroduces the defect. Reject.

recommended_option: Phase 1 (Changes 2a + 2b + 2c) first. Phase 2 (per-ID dedup) at operator's discretion, with ISS-2122 entry 1 investigation as the only urgent line item.
recommendation_rationale: Structural fix is cheap (~30 min) and pays compound interest (every future allocation is protected). Dedup is more expensive (~105 min) and one-shot — only valuable if operator-confusing queries are a current pain point. ISS-2122 entry 1 is the only collision row whose underlying work may be unfinished, so it deserves its own pass independent of the dedup batch.

counter_considerations.operator_perspective: The collisions are not actively breaking anything per the directive's own priority_note. A reviewer could argue PROP-063 is over-engineering — fix the 1 misrouted closure (ISS-2122 entry 1) manually and leave the rest. Response: Change 2a alone is 10 min and prevents ALL future occurrences. Even if Phase 2 dedup is skipped, Phase 1 is high-ROI and low-risk.
counter_considerations.could_the_self_heal_break_anything: The self-heal can only INCREASE next_id, never decrease. The worst-case behavior is allocating an ID slightly higher than open.next_id, which is identical to what happens when a closed-issues append races an open-issues write. No downstream consumer assumes next_id is contiguous with open ID max — gaps are already legal (per expansion-tracker-gap-semantics.md, which documents similar gap rules for EXP).
counter_considerations.could_the_integrity_audit_false_positive: Change 2c flags ANY duplicate ID as MAJOR. If a legitimate dedup pass (Phase 2) writes a `rewritten_from` field to preserve audit trail, the audit must accept that as an acceptable shape. Implementation note: the duplicate-ID audit in Change 2c should match by id field only — once dedup is applied, duplicates are gone, audit returns clean. Until Phase 2 lands, the audit will report 29 IDs flagged — which is correct (it's a known backlog); the audit description should say 'see PROP-063 Step 3 dedup table' to prevent the daily noise from spawning new ISSs.
counter_considerations.risk_of_misrouting_unrecovered_audit_tra: Phase 2 rewrite (option ii: rewrite-in-place with rewritten_from field) preserves audit trail. Option (i) DELETE is simpler but loses provenance. The default recommendation (ii) trades 1 extra field per rewrite for full traceability.

implementation_steps: 1a. Edit monitor/prompts/reference/decider-reporting.md Step 5 → replace the existing 'Example: create a new issue' code block with the PROP-063 archive-aware self-heal block (Change 2a). Add the prose addendum after the existing 'Never scan for max ID across issues' warning explaining the PROP-063 distinction.
1b. Edit monitor/prompts/reference/decider-intake.md line 100 → expand the 'Step 5 conventions' reference per Change 2b.
1c. Edit monitor/prompts/structure-integrity.md → add the ISS duplicate-ID audit per Change 2c (after the EXP-tracker collision section).
1d. (Optional) Edit decider-reporting.md to add the same-run-guard per Change 2d.

1e. Verify: run decider in a synthetic 'rewound next_id' scenario (set open-issues.json.next_id back to 2000 manually, then trigger a decider intake — assert that the next allocation produces ISS-2215 or higher, not ISS-2001).
1f. Verify: next morning's integrity run should report 29 duplicate-ID rows. If it doesn't, the audit didn't wire. If it reports a different count, investigate.

1g. To prevent the daily audit from generating 29 new ISSs on its first run after Phase 1 lands, the audit should produce a SINGLE finding with description='ISS duplicate-ID rows: 29 IDs affected — tracked under PROP-063 Phase 2' AND tracked_under='PROP-063'. Use the existing PROP-tracking convention.
PHASE 2 — Retroactive dedup (operator-applied at discretion, ~105 min total):

2a. Investigate ISS-2122 entry 1 first (live-state cleanup pipeline bug): grep EXP-426 + 'live-state cleanup' + 'attention-inbox' + 'state-mutation pipeline' against closed-issues.json and analyst-reports/. If a downstream legitimate fix exists, repoint entry 1's closure_reason. If not, restore status='assigned-tinker'.
2b. Apply the 4 A-class dedups (ISS-1423, ISS-1864, ISS-2154, ISS-2173). Use the per-ID disposition table in Step 3.
2c. Apply the 2 A-hybrid dedups (ISS-2120, ISS-2121). Drop the A-class duplicate entry; rewrite the B-class collision entry to a new ID.

2d. Apply the 21 pure-B rewrites (ISS-1819, 1834, 1857-1863, 1865-1868, 1922, 1967, 2016, 2124, 2200-2203). For each, allocate the next ISS-{nextAvail} from the now-hardened allocator, append `rewritten_from` field.
2e. Apply ISS-2122 E2 rewrite and ISS-2123 dispositions.
2f. After Phase 2, run the integrity audit. Expected output: no duplicate-ID findings.

2g. Update the PROP-063 status field to 'implemented' with implemented_at + implemented_by + implementation_note ('Phase 1 applied {date}; Phase 2 applied {date}' or partial-Phase notes if operator stopped early).
implementation_pseudocode_summary: See Change 2a (allocator hardening), Change 2c (integrity audit). Both are <50 lines of additive JS code; no schema changes, no file moves.

edge_cases.closed_issues_json_unreadable_or_malform: The self-heal block uses try/catch and falls back to maxClosedNum=0. In that case the allocator behaves like the pre-PROP-063 behavior (using open.next_id directly). NOT a regression — equivalent to old behavior under the same fault condition.
edge_cases.closure_ledger_jsonl_missing: Same — try/catch fallback. The ledger is informational for the self-heal; closed-issues.json is the authoritative max-ID source.
edge_cases.non_standard_id_formats_like_iss_win062c: Regex `^ISS-(\d+)$` excludes non-standard IDs. They won't contribute to maxClosedNum, but they also can't collide with allocated standard IDs (different format), so safe.
edge_cases.concurrent_decider_runs: Theoretically possible if operator triggers an ad-hoc run during a cron run. The self-heal cannot prevent this because both runs read the same closed-issues.json snapshot. The same-run-guard (Change 2d) provides marginal protection but the real fix is operator-discipline + clone-uniqueness per CLAUDE.md 'Per-session clone discipline'. Document this as a known residual risk.

idempotency_contract: Allocator self-heal is idempotent: running the max-walk on the same state always yields the same `safeNext`. The next_id increment is the only mutation, and it's atomic per-write. Integrity audit is read-only and idempotent. The Phase 2 rewrite is NOT idempotent (each rewrite consumes one new ID) and must be applied exactly once per duplicate-ID-entry-pair.

downstream_consumers: all decider runs (use the new allocator)
next day's integrity report (gains the duplicate-ID audit)
verify-pending-state.js (will see fewer ambiguous id matches once dedup is applied)
blast_radius.no_breaking_changes: Schema unchanged; field shapes unchanged; existing consumers see the same files with strictly-better contents.

operator_action_required: Phase 1: 3 prompt edits + 1 verification run (~30 min). Safe to apply standalone.
Phase 2: per-ID dedup pass (~105 min) — at operator discretion. Recommend investigating ISS-2122 entry 1 first (~15 min) regardless of whether full Phase 2 happens.
rejection_criteria: If operator concludes the 29 collisions are below-noise (no actionable query confusion) AND the May-18 disaster cluster is a one-off (no future risk), then Phase 1 alone is sufficient. Reject Phase 2 dedup as over-engineering.

If operator wants to defer ALL action: low-priority directive permits this — PROP-063 stays 'proposed' as the design-of-record for if/when collisions accumulate further. The directive marks 'completed' upon PROP-063 authoring; implementation timing is operator's call.
related_props: PROP-022 (phase 5 EXP archive-aware self-heal — the pattern PROP-063 mirrors)
PROP-037 (integrity-finding ISS-creation generalization — the same intake path)
PROP-059 (mandatory verification_pattern — touches closed-issues.json write path)
PROP-062 (verification-pattern defects — explicitly punted this class to PROP-063)
estimated_implementation_effort: Phase 1: 30 min operator + Opus prompt edits. Phase 2: 105 min operator-applied edits to closed-issues.json with audit-trail preservation. Total: ~135 min for both phases; ~30 min if Phase 1 only.
